spec_match.rationale: Renders a real `<usermedia>` element with a `<script type="permissionconstraints">` child, listens for `streamready` / `error`, and reads `element.stream` — exactly the spec contract.

interactive.rationale: Side-by-side panels: a JS getUserMedia button and the declarative `<usermedia>` element are both clickable. HTMLUserMediaElement feature-detection dot updates live. Step-by-step comparison panels explain each contrast.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises a side-by-side comparison showing constraints, events, and stream lifecycle — delivered with three step panels.

edge_cases.rationale: Happy path on both sides is well covered, but the error branch (`onerror`) is wired in markup yet never reached in the UI. Also no demonstration of repeated denial (the explicit recovery path the explainer highlights) or what happens when the element is hidden via CSS (the browser is supposed to refuse to honour clicks).

design_system.rationale: All colours via CSS variables, no glyph icons, button colour override paired with background.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate the recovery-from-denied path
openQuestions.detail: The whole motivation behind `<usermedia>` is that users who previously denied can recover via the element click without diving into chrome://settings. Add a `simulate previously denied` toggle (or instructions to click Deny first) and show the element still being clickable and recovering on a subsequent click.

openQuestions.title: Wire a visible error path
openQuestions.detail: `onerror="umError()"` exists but the page has no way to drive an error — visitors with cameras will only ever see streamready. Add a `simulate denied` button that calls `dispatchEvent(new Event('error'))` (or that uses `navigator.permissions.revoke()` if available) so the second branch is observable.

openQuestions.title: Show what happens when the element is hidden
openQuestions.detail: The spec deliberately ignores clicks on `<usermedia>` if the element is not user-visible. Add a `hide via display:none` toggle and document that the click is rejected by the browser — that anti-spoofing property is the security story.

openQuestions.title: Expose `userchangedenabled` in the live demo
openQuestions.detail: Step 3 of the comparison documents `userchangedenabled` but doesn't bind a listener in the actual element on this page. Wire one to a status line so a visitor can click the element's mute toggle and see the event fire.

summary: Strongest of the six concepts for this feature — real element next to real JS API, accurate step comparison, sound design. Two follow-ups: wire the error/denied path so the contrast story shows both sides, and probe the hidden-element security rule.
